description: Wrap marketing content inside a desktop, editor, or docs-like shell so the homepage feels native to a technical product environment.
evidence_notes: PostHog uses file labels, editor-like chrome, and tool-shaped framing to make the homepage feel product-native.
rules: Use the shell to reinforce audience context, not to hide the product message.
Keep the fake desktop or editor framing structurally simple.
Preserve clear semantic reading order even when the surface looks like an app.
implementation_notes: Build the frame from standard layout primitives, not inaccessible layered divs.
Use labels, tabs, file chips, or toolbars sparingly to avoid parody.
Test keyboard focus order and heading hierarchy carefully because decorative chrome can confuse screen-reader and tab flows.
avoid_when: The product audience is broad and not developer-native.
The shell makes the hero harder to scan than a plain layout would.
The frame is mostly a joke and adds little informational value.
